 III. Legal Standard on Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 12(b)(1) and 12(b)(6) 
4 
 Under Rule 12(b)(1) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, a defendant may seek 
5 
 to dismiss a complaint for lack of jurisdiction over the subject matter. The federal court 
6 
 is one of limited jurisdiction. See Gould v. Mutual Life Ins. Co. v. New York, 790 F.2d 
7 
 769, 774 (9th Cir. 1986). As such, it cannot reach the merits of any dispute until it 
8 
 confirms its own subject matter jurisdiction. See Steel Co. v. Citizens for a Better 
9 
 Environ., 523 U.S. 83, 95 (1998). When considering a Rule 12(b)(1) motion to dismiss, 
10 
 the district court is free to hear evidence regarding jurisdiction and to rule on that issue 
11 
 prior to trial, resolving factual disputes where necessary. See Augustine v. United States, 
12 
 704 F.2d 1074, 1077 (9th Cir. 1983). In such circumstances, "[n]o presumptive 
13 
 truthfulness attaches to plaintiff's allegations, and the existence of disputed facts will not 
14 
 preclude the trial court from evaluating for itself the merits of jurisdictional claims." Id. 
15 
 (quoting Thornhill Publishing Co. v. General Telephone & Electronic Corp., 594 F.2d 
16 
 730, 733 (9th Cir. 1979)). Plaintiff, as the party seeking to invoke jurisdiction, has the 
17 
 burden of establishing that jurisdiction exists. See Kokkonen v. Guardian Life Ins. Co. of 
18 
 Am., 511 U.S. 375, 377 (1994). 
19 
 Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) permits dismissal for "failure to state a 
20 
 claim upon which relief can be granted." Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(b)(6). Dismissal under Rule 
21 
 12(b)(6) is appropriate where the complaint lacks a cognizable legal theory or sufficient 
22 
 facts to support a cognizable legal theory. See Balistreri v. Pacifica Police Dep't., 901 
23 
 F.2d 696, 699 (9th Cir. 1990). Under Rule 8(a)(2), the plaintiff is required only to set

1 forth a "short and plain statement of the claim showing that the pleader is entitled to 
2 relief," and "give the defendant fair notice of what the . . . claim is and the grounds upon 
3 which it rests." Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 555 (2007). 
4 A complaint may survive a motion to dismiss only if, taking all well-pleaded 
5 factual allegations as true, it contains enough facts to "state a claim to relief that is 
6 plausible on its face." Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009) (quoting Twombly, 
7 550 U.S. at 570). "A claim has facial plausibility when the plaintiff pleads factual 
8 content that allows the court to draw the reasonable inference that the defendant is liable 
9 for the misconduct alleged." Id. "Threadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of 
10 action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice." Id. "In sum, for a 
11 complaint to survive a motion to dismiss, the non-conclusory factual content, and 
12 reasonable inferences from that content, must be plausibly suggestive of a claim entitling 
13 the plaintiff to relief." Moss v. U.S. Secret Serv., 572 F.3d 962, 969 (9th Cir. 2009) 
14 (quotations omitted). In reviewing a Rule 12(b)(6) motion, the Court accepts as true all 
15 facts alleged in the complaint, and draws all reasonable inferences in favor of the 
16 plaintiff. al-Kidd v. Ashcroft, 580 F.3d 949, 956 (9th Cir. 2009). The Court evaluates 
17 lack of statutory standing under the Rule 12(b)(6) standard. Maya v. Centex Corp., 658 
18 F.3d 1060, 1067 (9th Cir. 2011). 
19 Where a motion to dismiss is granted, "leave to amend should be granted ‘unless 
20 the court determines that the allegation of other facts consistent with the challenged 
21 pleading could not possibly cure the deficiency.'" DeSoto v. Yellow Freight Sys., Inc., 
22 957 F.2d 655, 658 (9th Cir. 1992) (quoting Schreiber Distrib. Co. v. Serv-Well Furniture 
23 Co., 806 F.2d 1393, 1401 (9th Cir. 1986)). In other words, where leave to amend would 
24 be futile, the Court may deny leave to amend. See Desoto, 957 F.2d at 658; Schreiber, 
25 806 F.2d at 1401.

16 A. Rooker-Feldman Doctrine 
17 As courts of original jurisdiction, federal district courts lack jurisdiction to review 
18 the final determinations of a state court in judicial proceedings. See Doe & Assocs. Law 
19 Offices v. Napolitano, 252 F.3d 1026, 1029 (9th Cir. 2001). However, federal district 
20 courts do have jurisdiction over a "general constitutional challenge," i.e., one that does 
21 not require review of a final state court decision in a particular case. Id. (citing Dubinka 
22 v. Judges of the Superior Court, 23 F.3d 218, 221 (9th Cir. 1994)). This distinction 
23 between a permissible general constitutional challenge and an impermissible appeal of a 
24 state court determination may be subtle and difficult to make. See id. (citing Worldwide 
25 Church of God v. Mcnair, 805 F.2d 888, 891 (9th Cir. 1986)). To draw out this 
26 distinction, the court must ask whether it is "in essence being called upon to review the 
27 state court decision." Id. A de facto appeal exists "when the federal plaintiff both asserts 
28 as her injury legal error or errors by the state court and seeks as her remedy relief from 
1 the state court judgment." Kougasian v. TMSL, Inc., 359 F.3d 1136, 1139-40 (9th Cir. 
2 2004). Once the court has found a de facto appeal, it must identify and decline to 
3 exercise jurisdiction over any issues that are "inextricably intertwined" with that appeal. 
4 Doe, 415 F.3d at 1043 (9th Cir. 2005). If the federal constitutional claims presented to 
5 the district court are ‘inextricably interwined' with the state court's judgement, then 
6 [plaintiff] is essentially asking the district court to review the state court's decision, 
7 which the district court may not do." Id. If "consideration and decision have been 
8 accomplished, action in federal court is an impermissible appeal from the state court 
9 decision." Id. (citing Worldwide Church of God, 805 F.2d at 892). "Where the district 
10 court must hold that the state court was wrong in order to find in favor of the plaintiff, the 
11 issues presented to both courts are inextricably intertwined." Id. 
12 The Ninth Circuit has cautioned against a broad application of the Rooker-Feldman 
13 doctrine and has noted that its application tends to be limited to the following types of 
14 claims: (1) when the plaintiff complains of harm caused by a state court judgment that 
15 directly withholds a benefit from (or imposes a detriment on) the plaintiff based on an 
16 allegedly erroneous ruling by the state court; or (2) when the plaintiff complains of a 
17 legal injury caused by a state court judgment, based on an allegedly erroneous legal 
18 ruling, in a case in which the plaintiff was one of the litigants. Noel v. Hall, 341 F.3d 
19 1148, 1163 (9th Cir. 2003). 
20 The analysis of Rooker-Feldman in Carmona v. Carmona, 603 F.3d 1041, 1051 
21 (9th Cir. 2010) is instructive. There, the plaintiff claimed that qualified domestic 
22 relations orders issued by a family court were based upon an erroneous application of 
23 ERISA. Accordingly, the plaintiff sought relief from the state court orders and the 
24 dismissal of a Family Court order, and prayed for the federal district court to order that 
25 the proceedings in family court be dismissed with prejudice and to enjoin enforcement of 
26 
27 
28 
1 the orders. The Ninth Circuit found that Rooker-Feldman applied given that the plaintiff 
2 asked the district court to set aside the state court orders.

11 B. Standing Pursuant to 12(b)(1) 
12 Defendants allege that Plaintiff does not have standing to pursue the ERISA claims 
13 because he has failed to assert an injury in fact. Plaintiff counters that he has standing 
14 under ERISA, 29 U.S.C. §§ 1001, Et Seq. 
15 "To establish standing to sue under ERISA, [plaintiffs] must show that they are 
16 plan ‘participants.'" Poore v. Simpson Paper Co., 566 F.3d 922, 925 (9th Cir. 2009). 
17 Under Section 502(a)(2), standing is granted to a plan participant to bring an action 
18 against a defendant who breaches a fiduciary duty with respect to that plan. See 29 
19 U.S.C. § 1132(a)(2). An ERISA plan participant is "any employee or former employee 
20 of an employer . . . who is or may become eligible to receive a benefit of any type from 
21 an employee benefit plan which covers employees of such employer." 29 U.S.C. § 
22 1002(7). 
23 "To bring an ERISA lawsuit, a plaintiff must not only have standing under the 
24 statute, but must also meet the standing requirements of Article III of the U.S. 
25 Constitution." Wells v. California Physicians' Serv., No. C05-01229 CRB, 2007 WL 
26 926490, at *3 (N.D. Cal. Mar. 26, 2007) (citing cases). "[T]he irreducible constitutional 
27 minimum of [Article III] standing" contains three elements, namely, "[t]he plaintiff must 
28 have (1) suffered an injury in fact, (2) that is fairly traceable to the challenged conduct of 
1 the defendant, and (3) that is likely to be redressed by a favorable judicial 
2 decision." Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins, 136 S. Ct. 1540, 1547 (2016) (citing Lujan v. Defs. of 
3 Wildlife, 504 U.S. 555, 560 (1992)). 
4 "To establish injury in fact, a plaintiff must show that he or she suffered ‘an 
5 invasion of a legally protected interest' that is ‘concrete and particularized' and ‘actual or 
6 imminent, not conjectural or hypothetical.'" Id. at 1548 (quoting Lujan, 504 U.S. at 560). 
7 An injury may be "concrete" even if it is intangible, and "in determining whether an 
8 intangible harm constitutes injury in fact, both history and the judgment of Congress play 
9 important roles." Id. at 1549. The judgment of Congress is "instructive and important" 
10 because "Congress is well positioned to identify intangible harms that meet minimum 
11 Article III requirements." Id. Thus, "Congress has the power to define injuries and 
12 articulate chains of causation that will give rise to a case or controversy where none 
13 existed before." Id. (quoting Lujan, 504 U.S. at 580 (Kennedy, J., concurring)). 
14 Nonetheless, "Congress' role in identifying and elevating intangible harms does not mean 
15 that a plaintiff automatically satisfies the injury-in-fact requirement whenever a statute 
16 grants a person a statutory right and purports to authorize that person to sue to vindicate 
17 that right." Id. at 1549. Thus, while a procedural violation "can be sufficient in some 
18 circumstances to constitute injury in fact," for example, where there is a "risk of real 
19 harm," a "bare procedural violation, divorced from any concrete harm" does not "satisfy 
20 the injury-in-fact requirement of Article III." Id. (emphasis added). Cf. Harris v. Amgen, 
21 Inc., 573 F.3d 728, 733 (9th Cir. 2009) (plaintiffs had standing under ERISA to recover 
22 losses occasioned by breach of fiduciary duty based on the plan administrators' purchase 
23 of stocks with artificially inflated prices); Ziegler v. Connecticut Gen. Life Ins. Co., 916 
24 F.2d 548, 551 (9th Cir. 1990) (plaintiffs need not allege injuries for certain ERISA 
25 violations, including a plan fiduciary's failure to perform for the exclusive benefit of 
26 participants and their beneficiaries). In CIGNA, the Supreme Court maintained a required 
27 showing of "actual harm" with respect to the question of standing. CIGNA Corp. v. 
28 Amara, 563 U.S. 421, 444 (2011).

3 1. Rule 11 
4 Rule 11 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure ("Rule 11") imposes a duty on 
5 attorneys to certify that (1) they have read the pleadings or the motions they file, and (2) 
6 the pleading or motion is well-grounded in fact, has a colorable basis in law, and is not 
7 filed for an improper purpose. Sec. Farms. v. Int'l Bhd. Of Teamsters, 124 F.3d 999, 1016 
8 (9th Cir. 1997) (citing Rule 11(b)). Generally, sanctions are appropriately imposed on an 
9 attorney for a filing "if either a) the paper is filed for an improper purpose, or b) the paper 
10 is ‘frivolous.' " Townsend v. Holman Consulting Corp., 929 F.2d 1358, 1362 (9th Cir. 
11 1990) (quoting Zaldivar v. City of Los Angeles, 780 F.2d 823, 832 (9th Cir. 1986)). 
12 However, as the Ninth Circuit has recognized, a special set of considerations 
13 pertain to Rule 11 motions directed at complaints. Unlike other filings, complaints may 
14 be challenged only for "frivolousness," which the Ninth Circuit uses as a "shorthand ... to 
15 denote a filing that is both baseless and made without a reasonable and competent 
16 inquiry." Id. Unlike the "improper purpose" inquiry, "frivolousness" is not concerned 
17 with the motivations of the signing attorney, and "subjective evidence of the signer's 
18 purpose is to be disregarded" so long as the contested papers are not 
19 baseless. Id. According to the Ninth Circuit, complaints, which serve as the legal 
20 "vehicle through which [the plaintiff] enforces his substantive legal rights," should be 
21 preserved to the extent possible, since the successful vindication of "those rights benefits 
22 not only individual plaintiffs but may benefit the public." Id. 
23 Frivolousness is determined objectively. "[T]he subjective intent of the ... movant 
24 to file a meritorious document is of no moment. The standard is reasonableness. The 
25 ‘reasonable [person]' against which conduct is tested is a competent attorney admitted to 
26 practice before the district court." G.C. and K.B. Invest., Inc. v. Wilson, 326 F.3d 1096, 
27 1109 (9th Cir. 2003). At base, "[t]he issue in determining whether to 
28 impose sanctions under Rule 11 is whether a reasonable attorney, having conducted an 
1 objectively reasonable inquiry into the facts and law, would have concluded that the 
2 offending paper was well-founded." Truesdell v. So. Cal. Permanente Med. Grp., 209 
3 F.R.D. 169, 174 (C.D. Cal. 2002). 
4 Cases warranting imposition of sanctions for frivolous actions are "rare and 
5 exceptional." Operating Eng'rs Pension Trust v. A–C Co., 859 F.2d 1336, 1344 (9th Cir. 
6 1988); In re Keegan Mgmt. Co. Sec. Litig., 78 F.3d 431, 437 (9th Cir. 
7 1996) (characterizing sanctions as "an extraordinary remedy, one to be exercised with 
8 extreme caution"). "District courts enjoy much discretion in determining whether and 
9 how much sanctions are appropriate." Oliver v. In-N-Out Burgers, 945 F. Supp. 2d 1126, 
10 1130 (S.D. Cal. 2013) (citing Haynes v. City & Cnty. of San Francisco, 688 F.3d 984, 
11 987 (9th Cir. 2012)). A district court's determination of whether to enter sanctions is 
12 reviewed for abuse of discretion. Id. 
13 Here, Young bears the burden of demonstrating that the FAC includes sanctionable 
14 material. The FAC presented genuine questions of law and Plaintiff alleged significant 
15 stakes since the sum of Plaintiffs' funds transferred to Young totaled to over $3 million. 
16 FAC ¶ 57. Having discerned no sanctionable material during the course of its review of 
17 the FAC, the Court DENIES Young's Rule 11 motion. 
18 2. 28 U.S.C. § 1927 
19 Pursuant to 28 U.S.C. § 1927, "[a]ny attorney ... who so multiplies the proceedings 
20 in any case unreasonably and vexatiously may be required by the court to satisfy 
21 personally the excess costs, expenses, and attorneys' fees reasonably incurred because of 
22 such conduct." "[S]ection 1927 sanctions must be supported by a finding of subjective 
23 bad faith," which "is present when an attorney knowingly or recklessly raises 
24 a frivolous argument, or argues a meritorious claim for the purpose of harassing an 
25 opponent." In re Keegan Mgmt. Co., Sec. Lit., 78 F.3d 431, 436 (9th Cir.1996) (internal 
26 quotations omitted) (emphasis added). 
27 The Ninth Circuit has found that while recklessness may be the standard under § 
28 1927, "it is an insufficient basis for sanctions under a court's inherent power." In re 
1 Keegan Mgmt. Co., Sec. Litig., 78 F.3d 431, 436 (9th Cir. 1996). Instead, counsel's 
2 conduct must "constitute[ ] or [be] tantamount to bad faith." Roadway Express, Inc. v. 
3 Piper, 447 U.S. 752, 767, 100 S.Ct. 2455, 2465, 65 L.Ed.2d 488 (1980). In sanctioning 
4 counsel, "[c]ourts may not invoke [inherent] powers without a ‘specific finding of bad 
5 faith.' " Yagman v. Republic Ins., 987 F.2d 622, 628 (9th Cir.1993) (quoting United 
6 States v. Stoneberger, 805 F.2d 1391, 1393 (9th Cir.1986)); accord Zambrano v. City of 
7 Tustin, 885 F.2d 1473, 1478 (9th Cir.1989) ("To insure that restraint is properly 
8 exercised, we have routinely insisted upon a finding of bad faith before sanctions may be 
9 imposed under the court's inherent power."). "Bad faith is present when an attorney 
10 knowingly or recklessly raises a frivolous argument or argues a meritorious claim for the 
11 purpose of harassing an opponent." Trulis v. Barton, 107 F.3d 685, 694 (9th Cir. 1995). 
12 Here, Young asserts that Plaintiff's failure to add facts in his amended complaint to 
13 address the issues raised in Young's original mooted motion to dismiss is proof of 
14 Plaintiff's attempt to multiply the proceedings. Young's filing of the amended complaint 
15 is insufficient to constitute bad faith given that the original motion to dismiss had not 
16 been ruled on. In Stone Creek, the Ninth Circuit affirmed the lower court's sanctions 
17 order since the plaintiff failed to drop withdraw its actual damages claim when the 
18 plaintiff knew that its actual damages claim was meritless, and plaintiff intended to 
19 pursue only the defendant's profits. Stone Creek, Inc. v. Omnia Italian Design, Inc., 875 
20 F.3d 426, 443 (9th Cir. 2017), cert. denied, 138 S. Ct. 1984, 201 L. Ed. 2d 248 (2018). In 
21 the district court proceedings, the plaintiff opposed the defendant's motion to vacate the 
22 jury trial and caused additional expense and delay. Stone Creek Inc. v. Omnia Italian 
23 Design Inc., No. CV-13-00688-PHX-DLR, 2016 WL 492629, at *2 (D. Ariz. Feb. 9, 
24 2016), aff'd, 862 F.3d 1131 (9th Cir. 2017), and aff'd, 875 F.3d 426 (9th Cir. 2017). As a 
25 result of the plaintiff's opposition, the defendant was "forced to expend time and 
26 resources defending against the claim by, for example, taking depositions and having its 
27 expert prepare a report." Stone Creek, 875 F.3d at 443. 
28 
1 Here, Plaintiff has not engaged in comparable behavior to support a finding of bad 
2 faith. The Court therefore DENIES Young's 28 U.S.C. § 1927 motion.4
